---
title: ""
page-layout: full
---

::: {.project-header}
::: {.container}

[← Back to Research](../research.qmd){.back-link}

# Predicting Strike vs. Protest Events

::: {.project-meta}
<span><strong>Course:</strong> Big Data: Policy, Governing & Management</span>
<span><strong>Instructor:</strong> Prof. Rod Albuyeh</span>
<span><strong>Term:</strong> Fall 2025</span>
<span><strong>Tools:</strong> R · caret · XGBoost · Random Forest · pROC</span>
:::

:::
:::

::: {.container style="max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; padding-bottom: 5rem;"}

## The Question

Can we predict whether a U.S. labor action event will be classified as a **strike** versus a **protest** — and which economic and geographic conditions are most predictive?

## Data

**Labor Action Tracker (LAT)** from Cornell's School of Industrial Labor Relations, covering 2021–2025. Merged at ZIP/ZCTA level with:

- **DCI** — Distressed Communities Index (2015–2023)
- **SDI** — Social Deprivation Index (2015–2019)
- **IRS tax statistics** — 2022 income aggregates by ZIP

Final modeling dataset: thousands of events with complete predictor coverage after geographic join validation (>98% ZCTA coverage).

## Methodology

**Data pipeline highlights:**
- Temporal train/test split (train: pre-Dec 2023; test: Dec 2023–Jan 2025) — never let future data touch training
- Saved factor levels exclusively from training data and applied consistently to test set
- Only filtered on missing outcome variable — never on predictors
- Built 20+ binary industry indicators from semicolon-delimited tags
- Log-transformed participant counts with median imputation on training data only

**Models compared:**

| Model | Approach |
|---|---|
| Logistic Regression | Baseline interpretable model |
| Random Forest | Ensemble, handles nonlinearity |
| XGBoost | Gradient boosting, best AUC |

**Evaluation:** AUC/ROC curves, confusion matrices, variable importance plots.

## Key Outputs

::: {.project-visual}
*[ROC curves comparing three models — add your image here]*
::: {.visual-caption}
ROC curves for logistic regression, Random Forest, and XGBoost on held-out test set.
:::
:::

::: {.project-visual}
*[Variable importance plot — add your image here]*
::: {.visual-caption}
Variable importance from final model. Public sector industry indicators and geographic distress indices emerge as key predictors.
:::
:::

## Code & Files

[View on GitHub](https://github.com/javramis){.cta-secondary style="padding: 0.6rem 1.4rem; border: 1.5px solid #1a1a2e; color: #1a1a2e; border-radius: 2px; font-size: 0.85rem; letter-spacing: 0.08em; text-transform: uppercase; text-decoration: none; display: inline-block;"}

:::
